exemplar.title: Assessing the Environmental and Economic Impact of Sharing Economy in Beijing, China

detailedExemplarData.title: Assessing the Environmental and Economic Impact of Sharing Economy in Beijing, China

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated and effectively linked to the subsequent discussion, providing a coherent focal point.
The methodology section justifies the choice of time frame and reliance on secondary sources, demonstrating informed selection.
The topic of the sharing economy in Beijing is communicated accurately with an appropriate purpose and focus for systematic investigation.
weaknesses: The triple focus on efficiency, sustainability and well-being dilutes precision and limits depth of analysis.
Methodology lacks detail on selection criteria, triangulation of sources and any primary research, reducing transparency.
Introducing new issues (e.g. labour-market threats) in the conclusion without prior analysis weakens coherence and focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work consistently meets the descriptors for band 3â4: the topic is communicated accurately with clear purpose, the research question is well framed and linked to discussion, and the methodology is generally appropriate with some evidence of informed source selection. However, the breadth of focus and lack of methodological detail prevent a higher band. A score of 4/6 accurately reflects this performance.

strengths: Key economic concepts (externalities, subsidies, AD/AS analysis) are clearly and coherently explained.
Economic terminology and diagrams are used correctly and consistently throughout.
Source material such as DALYs application and participation statistics are appropriately linked to arguments.
weaknesses: There are occasional imprecisions and mislabelling (e.g. conflating âproduction valueâ with âtransaction sizeâ, mislabelling LRAS).
Integration of some sources is not consistently critical, and some evidence is outdated or not Beijing-specific.
Minor lapses in diagram labelling and citation formatting reduce overall precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge of economic theory with mostly accurate terminology and relevant application of source material. Occasional inaccuracies and limited critical integration of sources keep the work in the upper half of the 3â4 band. Awarding 4/6 reflects solid competence without meeting the consistently high precision of the top band.

strengths: A substantial body of research is marshalled, with the majority of evidence appropriately relevant to the research question.
Analysis applies standard economic models logically, and conclusions are generally supported by the evidence presented.
The argument is reasoned and acknowledges limitations, showing an attempt at critical evaluation.
weaknesses: Analysis lacks quantitative depth (e.g. elasticity estimates, carbon footprint data) and alternative explanations are underexplored.
Evaluation remains surface-level, without sustained critical synthesis or long-run policy trade-off discussion.
Reliance on secondary media and consultancy sources reduces consistency of research relevance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs critical thinking aligns with the 4â6 band descriptors: the work demonstrates clear analysis with mostly supported conclusions, and a reasoned argument with partially critical evaluation. Shallow depth and uneven evidence prevent a higher band. A score of 4/12 reflects performance at the lower end of this band.

strengths: The investigation follows a logical chapter structure with a detailed table of contents that guides the reader.
Headings and sub-headings are used effectively to organise sections.
weaknesses: Essential IB elements (abstract, page numbers, appendices) are missing, undermining formal requirements.
Figures often lack labelled axes or units, and citation formatting is inconsistent.
Layout conventions such as headers/footers and uniform spacing are not fully applied.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the 1â2 band: structure is generally appropriate but missing key IB components, and several layout and formatting considerations are incorrectly applied or omitted. A score of 2/4 accurately reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question appears prominently and is referenced across sections, giving the essay a coherent thread. It is phrased clearly and linked to subsequent discussion. Nonetheless, its triple focus (efficiency, sustainability and well-being) dilutes precision; each dimension is treated more broadly than deeply. This partial rather than full focus aligns with the 3-4 band where clarity is evident but tight focus is only partially achieved.

The student presents the concept of the sharing-economy in Beijing with clear contextualisation and a logical rationale for its study. The introductory sections state the purpose (to assess efficiency, environmental outcomes and economic well-being) and show why the topic is pertinent for Economics. However, bundling three substantial outcome variables into one investigation stretches focus and inevitably limits depth, so communication is adequate rather than sharply refined. Overall, the topic is conveyed accurately and remains suitable for systematic investigation, meeting the 3â4 band descriptors.

Most charts, policy documents and academic commentary are clearly linked to arguments, and citations are provided. Nonetheless a share of evidence comes from dated reports (e.g., 2015 growth forecasts) or broad national data rather than Beijing-specific studies, weakening precision. Integration of sources is competent but not consistently critical, aligning with the âmostly relevant and appropriateâ level of the 3â4 band.

The essay follows a logical chapter structure with a detailed contents page, headings and sub-headings that guide the reader. However, essential IB elements such as an abstract and appendices are missing, and page numbers are absent. These omissions indicate only âgenerally appropriateâ structure with notable gaps, matching the 1â2 band; a score of 2 reflects the stronger end of that band.

While figures are captioned and cited, layout conventions are inconsistently applied: page numbering is absent, some diagrams lack labelled axes or units, and headers/footers are missing. Formatting of citations is uneven and spacing occasionally irregular. These shortcomings reveal that several key layout considerations are missing or incorrectly applied, situating the work at the lower end of the 1â2 band.

The discussion balances benefits and drawbacks and acknowledges data limitations, showing an attempt at evaluation. However, critiques remain surface-level and do not fully explore long-run implications or policy trade-offs. The argument is coherent but lacks sustained critical depth or synthesis characteristic of the top bands. This aligns with a âreasoned argument with partially critical evaluationâ in the 4â6 band.

Analytical sections apply standard economic models (AD/AS, externalities, subsidy analysis) to interpret trends. Reasoning is generally logical and conclusions are tied to evidence. Yet depth is limited: quantitative estimates (e.g., elasticity, DWL magnitude) are absent and alternative explanations (e.g., metro expansion) receive minimal treatment. The analysis is therefore âclearâ¦ with mostly supported conclusionsâ but not sufficiently penetrating for higher bands.

The essay demonstrates solid understanding of key economic conceptsâexternalities, subsidies, AD/AS analysisâand links them to the sharing-economy case. Explanations are largely accurate and coherent. Occasional imprecision (e.g., conflating âproduction valueâ with âtransaction sizeâ) and limited exploration of advanced theory prevent a move to the 5-6 band, situating the work comfortably within the competent 3-4 range.

The student amasses an appreciable body of statistics, reports and academic analyses that are generally pertinent to the question. Dependence on consultancy blogs and media items, plus gaps in Beijing-specific or primary data, reduce the consistency of relevance. Nevertheless, the majority of evidence speaks directly to efficiency or environmental impacts, warranting placement in the 4â6 band at the lower end.

The student outlines time frame, data types and rationale for relying on foreign consultancies because of domestic data opacity. Sources are largely secondary and no primary research is attempted, yet the choice is justified. While a reasonable range of statistics, policy papers and news items is used, criteria for inclusion, triangulation or diagram construction are not fully specified. Thus methodology shows informed but only partially systematic selection, fitting the âgenerally relevantâ¦ some evidence of informed selectionâ descriptor in the 3â4 band.

Economic terminologyâpositive externalities, welfare loss, subsidies, AD/AS, DALYsâis used correctly and consistently throughout the essay, with diagrams reinforcing conceptual discussion. Minor lapses in labelling (missing units on some axes) and a few informal word choices limit precision, but overall use of economic language is mostly accurate, meriting the upper end of the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: The limitations section candidly acknowledges reliability issues with Chinese government data, reflecting critical evaluation.
Reflection on methodology remains descriptive; discussing alternative approaches or primary data options would improve depth.
Employing DALYs to quantify health benefits demonstrates an advanced application of non-economic metrics to assess externalities.
Absence of carbon footprint analysis for bicycle manufacturing overlooks a key sustainability consideration.
The commentary on ventilation benefits for walkers is interesting but lacks direct linkage to Beijingâs urban environment data.
The discussion of vandalism and its impact on resource under-utilization shows critical awareness of real-world externalities.
Figure 1âs axes are unlabeled, omitting units and reducing interpretability of the transaction-volume data.
Page numbers are not included on the contents page, reducing clarity in cross-referencing and formal consistency.
The research question is effectively restated in the introduction, reinforcing the essayâs focus on efficiency, sustainability, and well-being.
Assertions about improved economic efficiency lack quantitative evidence or citation of specific empirical studies to support the claims.
The methodology section justifies the time frame (2018â2022) and explains reliance on secondary sources to mitigate domestic data opacity.
The methodology lacks detail on selection criteria and triangulation of sources, reducing transparency of the research design.
The definition and background of the sharing economy are clearly articulated, demonstrating coherent understanding of key concepts.
The list of shared devices would be more insightful if accompanied by data on relative market size or economic impact of each category.
This section cites âbicycle graveyardsâ qualitatively but does not provide data on the scale in Beijing, weakening evidence of its economic effect.
Figure 5âs supply-demand graph omits axis labels and units, reducing precision in interpreting subsidy effects.
The title is clearly stated at the start of the report, adhering to IB conventions and immediately informing the reader of the investigationâs focus.
The research question is prominently displayed and precisely frames the scope of the analysis, providing a coherent focal point for the essay.
An abstract section is missing from this report. Including an abstract would strengthen the formal structure and meet IB guidelines.
A word count is provided below the research question, confirming compliance with the 4 000-word limit requirement.
Page numbering is absent from this page header, which impedes navigation and does not meet formatting expectations.
The detailed table of contents clearly outlines the structure of the investigation, facilitating reader navigation through the sections.
The Keynesian diagram mislabels the LRAS curve as upward-sloping; long-run aggregate supply should be vertical.
Sustainability discussion relies on national COâ data without quantifying the direct impact of shared mobility in Beijing itself.
Contextualizing Beijingâs emissions within Chinaâs Paris Agreement commitments adds valuable policy-level relevance.
The historical summary of the sharing economy is descriptive but does not integrate foundational economic theory or models to frame the context.
Privacy risks are noted, but the link to environmental or economic outcomes (research question dimensions) is not fully developed.
The analysis of positive externalities integrates social cost-benefit logic effectively, illustrating welfare implications.
Labeling total transaction value as âproductionâ in economic-model terms conflates monetary turnover with output, misusing terminology.
The inclusion of participation statistics (800 million users, 84 million providers) effectively illustrates the sharing economyâs scale.
Figure 4âs classification of shared bikes under âmerit goodsâ is conceptually inaccurate; they are services, not merit goods with public-good characteristics.
Health benefit figures from European contexts are not critically assessed for applicability to Beijingâs demographic and infrastructure.
The section on government subsidies clearly identifies dependence on public support and the risk of subsidy abuse.
Works cited entries are inconsistently formatted and lack a uniform citation style; adopting one standard would enhance professionalism.
The conclusion effectively recaps key findings on efficiency, sustainability, and well-being, reinforcing the research question.
Introducing potential future labor-market threats in the conclusion without prior analysis reduces coherence and focus.
